title: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: David R. Adams, Camilo Toro, Joseph Loscalzo

items.subtopic: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology | THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE | THE MEANING AND CONTEXT OF A DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Understanding the Undiagnosed Disease State and Its Challenges
items.content: The undiagnosed disease state refers to conditions where, despite reasonable efforts using established methods, a diagnosis remains elusive. This prolonged diagnostic ambiguity presents unique challenges for patients, their families, and healthcare providers.
keyPoints: A patient with an undiagnosed disease has no discerned medical diagnosis after reasonable efforts.
The term 'disease' implies discomfort or abnormality not due to external injury.
Patients in this state endure prolonged periods without a clear diagnosis, impacting their care and quality of life.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology | THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE | THE MEANING AND CONTEXT OF A DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology | THE MEANING AND CONTEXT OF A DIAGNOSIS
items.title: The Evolving Specificity in Diagnosis with Precision Medicine
items.content: Diagnosis is becoming more specific due to advances in medical science, emphasizing precision medicine. This includes understanding disease context within genomic landscapes and environmental factors.
keyPoints: A diagnosis can have multiple levels of specificity: clinical, mechanistic, histopathologic, molecular.
Parkinson's disease diagnosis involves bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, postural instability, and response to L-dopa.
Precision medicine focuses on genomic landscape, environment, social factors, medical history, nutrition, and microbiome.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology | THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE | THE MEANING AND CONTEXT OF A DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION
items.title: Impact of Undiagnosed Rare Diseases on Patients and Healthcare Systems
items.content: Undiagnosed rare diseases, affecting an estimated 6-10% of the population globally, can lead to significant personal, familial, and societal challenges. The uncertainty surrounding these conditions often results in prolonged periods without a diagnosis, causing stress, decreased productivity, limited access to resources, and increased medical service utilization.
keyPoints: More than 6000 rare diseases affect millions worldwide.
Aggregate population prevalence of rare diseases is estimated at 6-10%.
Undiagnosed patients may experience decades without a diagnosis.
Consequences include stress, decreased productivity, and excess medical service utilization.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION

items.subtopic: UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION
items.title: Importance of Comprehensive Data Collection in Diagnosing Rare Diseases
items.content: Comprehensive data collection is crucial for diagnosing rare diseases, as it ensures a thorough understanding of the disease course and verifies critical diagnostic elements. Gathering all available medical records helps avoid incorrect diagnoses due to incomplete or inaccurate second-hand information.
keyPoints: Gathering all available medical data is essential for diagnosis.
Correcting misinformation from previous evaluations is crucial.
Data collection aids in reconstructing the disease's history and understanding diagnostic processes.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.title: Importance of Reassessing Diagnostic Logic in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: When evaluating patients with undiagnosed diseases, it is crucial to reassess past diagnostic logic and data. Historical exclusions based on outdated testing methods should be reconsidered with current state-of-the-art technologies.
keyPoints: Historical tests like transferrin isoelectric focusing for CDG may miss diagnoses now detectable by mass spectrometry and molecular approaches.
Reassessment of diagnostic logic can uncover new leads using modern technology.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.title: Advantages of Hypothesis-Driven Molecular Testing
items.content: Hypothesis-driven molecular testing targets specific heritable disorders, offering improved sensitivity and cost-effectiveness compared to broader sequencing studies.
keyPoints: Targeted gene panels with structural variant detection enhance diagnostic accuracy.
Updated panels may include newly discovered genes not in initial evaluations.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.title: Role of Agnostic Molecular Testing in Identifying New Disease-Gene Associations
items.content: Agnostic molecular testing uses broad platforms like exome or genome sequencing to explore all detectable diagnoses, potentially revealing new disease-gene associations.
keyPoints: Unrestricted search through the human genome identifies medically relevant DNA variants.
Expert review and curation of bioinformatic data are essential for interpreting results.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.title: Importance of Periodic Reevaluation in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: Periodic reevaluation is crucial for patients with undiagnosed diseases to potentially achieve a diagnosis, as evidenced by the ~30% diagnostic rate reported by the UDN. This process involves symptomatic care, support, and regular reassessment strategies that incorporate new medical insights, disease progression, and emerging symptoms.
keyPoints: The Undiagnosed Diseases Network (UDN) reports a ~30% overall diagnostic rate with periodic reevaluation.
Reevaluation should be guided by new knowledge, disease progression, and the emergence of new signs or symptoms.
Regular communication with patients is essential to maintain trust and manage expectations.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · PERIODIC REEVALUATION

items.title: Leveraging New Literature for Diagnostic Clues in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: Staying updated with current literature is vital for diagnosing undiagnosed diseases, as new disorders and gene associations can provide critical diagnostic insights. Utilizing phenotype-driven search tools like PubMed and OMIM can help identify potential diagnoses based on clinical features.
keyPoints: Newly reported disorders and disease-gene associations are key sources of diagnostic resolution.
Phenotype-driven searches (e.g., using terms like 'cardiomyopathy arthropathy diabetes hyperpigmentation') can link symptoms to specific disorders such as hemochromatosis.
Iterative application of literature search tools should be part of formal, periodic reevaluation.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · PERIODIC REEVALUATION

items.title: Optimizing Genetic Testing Strategies for Undiagnosed Patients
items.content: When evaluating undiagnosed patients, especially younger ones, genetic testing results may already exist. Reanalysis of these data should consider the age and quality of the study and reported phenotype at the time. Collaboration with laboratory directors can clarify next steps, including whether reanalysis is available.
keyPoints: Reanalysis of exome/genome data should account for study age and patient phenotype.
Consultation with laboratory directors helps determine utility of older genetic test results.
Review DNA variants of unknown significance with updated gene reports.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · GENOMICS

items.title: The Role of Family Members in Genetic Testing
items.content: Including parents and siblings in new sequencing tests can provide significant insights. This approach is particularly valuable for understanding inherited conditions, and discussions with experts or testing laboratories are recommended to optimize the strategy.
keyPoints: Inclusion of family members (parents/siblings) enhances value of genetic tests.
Family-based approaches aid in diagnosing inherited conditions.
Expert consultation advised for optimal sequencing test strategies.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · GENOMICS

items.title: Pathognomonic Disease Phenotypes Indicate Specific Toxicant Exposures
items.content: Certain rare diseases are pathognomonic for specific toxicants, such as mesothelioma with asbestos and clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina with intrauterine DES exposure.
keyPoints: Mesothelioma is specifically linked to asbestos exposure.
Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina is linked to intrauterine diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.title: Chemical Exposures and Disease Manifestations: Complexity and Latency
items.content: Chemical exposures often interact with lifestyle, host susceptibility, and nonchemical stressors to produce common diseases, unusual phenotypes, or atypical disease presentations. The latency period between exposure and onset can be decades.
keyPoints: Chemical toxicant exposures rarely produce unique phenotypes alone.
Diseases like asthma are common outcomes of chemical interactions with lifestyle factors.
Latency periods for diseases such as chemical carcinogenesis or atherothrombosis can span decades.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.title: Systematic Exposure History: A Key Tool in Assessing Chemical-Related Disease Risk
items.content: A systematic exposure history is crucial for assessing chemical-related disease risk, involving job and lifestyle histories, temporal symptom analysis, and potential biomonitoring.
keyPoints: There are no standardized instruments for exposure assessment; guidelines exist (Goldman and Peters, 1981).
Exposure assessments include job history, review of exposures at work/home/hobbies, and food frequency questionnaires.
Further evaluation involves identifying specific agents, quantitative environmental assessments, and etiologic plausibility.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.title: Leveraging Phenotypic and Genotypic Similarities in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: For patients with rare or undiagnosed diseases, identifying similarly affected individuals can significantly enhance research opportunities. This approach facilitates the discovery of common pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic strategies by connecting patients to consortia focused on related medical conditions.
keyPoints: Phenotypic aggregation allows connection with disease-specific consortia (e.g., leukodystrophies, autoinflammatory disorders).
Organizations like NORD and the Office of Rare Disease Research support patient cohorts based on biological mechanisms or pathways.
Examples include the United Mitochondrial Disease Foundation.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · ENGAGEMENT OF RESEARCH APPROACHES

items.title: Utilizing Data Sharing for Identifying Similar Cases in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: Data-sharing resources, such as gene-matching databases, are crucial for matching similar cases globally. These systems help identify additional cases of ultrarare or newly described conditions without compromising patient privacy.
keyPoints: GeneMatcher is a prominent database for single-gene submissions, covering almost 70% of all known protein-coding genes.
DECIPHER extends utility to larger structural variants.
These databases facilitate matching by gene name and phenotypic features while protecting personal health information.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · ENGAGEMENT OF RESEARCH APPROACHES

items.title: Importance of Comprehensive Medical Record Collection in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: For patients with chronically undiagnosed diseases, maintaining a comprehensive collection of medical records is crucial. This includes consultation notes, original laboratory results, and radiology studies (preferably electronic). These records facilitate consultations, second opinions, and transitions between primary providers.
keyPoints: Families may be charged fees for obtaining copies of old studies.
Continuing advances in record access are occurring.
Comprehensive medical records aid in consultation, second opinions, and provider transitions.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION

items.title: Multidisciplinary Approach to Managing Illness Behaviors and Expectations in Undiagnosed Diseases
items.content: Patients with undiagnosed diseases often experience significant psychological and social challenges, including uncertainty about heritability and its impact on reproductive decisions. These factors can be as disabling as the primary illness itself, necessitating an individualized and multidisciplinary approach to care.
keyPoints: Patients may present at any stage of the grieving process due to undiagnosed diseases.
Uncertainty affects abilities, work, relationships, autonomy, and financial security.
Reproductive age patients face uncertainty about disorder heritability and reproductive decisions.
A multidisciplinary approach is required to address these complex issues.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is the original meaning of the term 'patient' as used in medical contexts?
items.explanation: The term 'patient' originally means "the one who endures suffering."

items.explanation: Neuronal α-synuclein deposition and Lewy body pathology are associated with a histopathologic diagnosis.

items.explanation: A clinical diagnosis of Parkinson's disease is based on the progressive emergence of signs and symptoms such as bradykinesia, rigidity, asymmetric rest tremor, and postural instability.

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.question: Which of the following can be an unrecognized underlying illness in patients believed to have 'functional' or 'somatoform' disorders?
items.explanation: Patients with 'functional' or 'somatoform' disorders may have unrecognized underlying illnesses such as nonepileptic events (pseudo-seizures).
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.question: What is a potential benefit of using broad scope screening tools in the absence of concrete diagnostic leads?
options: They reduce the need for physical examinations.
items.explanation: Broad scope screening tools may prove beneficial in generating meaningful diagnostic hypotheses when there are no concrete diagnostic leads.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.question: What is a key factor to consider when determining whether new diagnostic testing is practical?
options: Prior probability of disease and available resources.
The number of previous tests conducted.
items.explanation: Prior probability of disease and available resources are key factors in determining the practicality of new diagnostic testing.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.question: Which molecular approach involves considering all detectable diagnoses, even those with a low pretest probability?
items.explanation: Agnostic molecular testing typically uses data from a broad platform and considers all detectable diagnoses, even those with low pretest probability.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.question: What was the overall diagnostic rate reported for the Undiagnosed Diseases Network (UDN), including periodic reevaluation of early enrollees?
items.explanation: The UDN's diagnostic rate, including periodic reevaluation, was reported as ~30%.

items.question: What is an essential component of the care plan for patients with undiagnosed diseases?

items.explanation: Communication with the patient is an essential component in managing undiagnosed diseases.

items.question: Which tool can be used to connect clinical signs and symptoms (phenotypes) to disorders?

items.explanation: The Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man website can be used to connect phenotypes to disorders.

items.question: Which factor should be considered first when reanalyzing previously obtained exome and genome data?
options: The age and quality of the study
The availability of raw DNA sequencing data
Inclusion of parents and siblings in new tests
items.explanation: Reanalysis should start with consideration of both the age and quality of the study.

items.question: What is a recommended step when discussing sequence results generated in a clinical laboratory?
options: Consulting Marwaha et al., 2022 for further reading
Discussing with the laboratory director about next steps
Requesting raw DNA sequencing data from the patient
Including parents and siblings of the proband
items.explanation: A discussion between the provider and the laboratory director often answers important questions.

items.question: What is a potential benefit of including the parents and siblings in new sequencing tests?
options: It reduces the cost of testing.
It provides enormous value in some situations.
It ensures data quality for reanalysis.
It guarantees identification of bioinformatics collaborators.
items.explanation: Inclusion of parents and siblings has the potential to provide enormous value in some situations.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · GENOMICS

items.question: Which of the following diseases is pathognomonic for exposure to diethylstilbestrol (DES)?
options: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina
items.explanation: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina is pathognomonic for exposure to diethylstilbestrol (DES).
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.question: What complicates the risk assessment of chemical-induced diseases?
options: Unique phenotypes produced by chemical exposures
Availability of standardized instruments for exposure history
Lack of exposure biomarkers and short half-life of some contaminants
Immediate onset of symptoms following exposure
items.explanation: The risk assessment is complicated by the lack of exposure biomarkers for many common environmental toxicants, the short half-life of some contaminants, and long latency periods between exposure and disease.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.question: What does a systematic exposure history help ascertain in patients?
items.explanation: A systematic exposure history is a powerful tool for ascertaining chemical-related disease risk.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in a multistep approach to exposure assessment?
options: Review of workplace Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDSs)
items.explanation: Genomic analysis is not typically included in the multistep approach to exposure assessment.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.question: Which organization is mentioned as a starting point for finding consortia dedicated to related medical presentations?
options: The Office of Rare Disease Research within the National Center for Advancing Translational Science
items.explanation: NORD is mentioned as a useful starting point for finding consortia dedicated to related medical presentations.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · ENGAGEMENT OF RESEARCH APPROACHES

items.question: What is the primary benefit of phenotypic aggregation in undiagnosed disease research?
options: It reduces the need for DNA sequencing.
It allows patients to connect with consortia invested in related medical presentations.
It eliminates the need for clinical trials.
items.explanation: Phenotypic aggregation can allow patients to connect with consortia invested in related medical presentations, encouraging research and improving chances of finding commonalities.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · ENGAGEMENT OF RESEARCH APPROACHES

items.question: What is the purpose of gene-matching databases like GeneMatcher?
options: To provide immediate diagnosis for patients.
To match similar cases submitted by clinicians and researchers without sharing personal health information.
To store all known protein-coding genes.
items.explanation: Gene-matching databases like GeneMatcher are designed to match similar cases submitted by clinicians and researchers, flagging matches without sharing the patient's personal health information.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · ENGAGEMENT OF RESEARCH APPROACHES

items.question: What is recommended for families of patients with chronically undiagnosed diseases to maintain?
options: An updated collection of medical records, including consultation notes and original laboratory results.
A list of potential diagnoses based on family history.
Regular consultations with a single primary provider only.
Electronic copies of all social media interactions related to the illness.
items.explanation: Families should maintain an updated collection of medical records, including consultation notes and original laboratory results, for effective management and transitions in care.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is a significant challenge faced by patients with undiagnosed diseases?
options: The certainty of having a successful therapy.
Coping with uncertainty, loss of abilities, and financial security.
Immediate access to all medical records without any fees.
items.explanation: Patients face challenges such as coping with uncertainty, loss of abilities, work, relationships, autonomy, and financial security.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is a potential concern for patients of reproductive age with undiagnosed diseases?
options: The availability of immediate therapy options.
The potential heritability of their disorder and its effects on future reproductive decisions.
Access to electronic copies of all medical records.
The ability to avoid any form of counseling.
items.explanation: Patients of reproductive age may be concerned about the potential heritability of their disorder, affecting future reproductive decisions and risks to children and relatives.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION
items.assertion: Being undiagnosed has profound detrimental effects on patients.
items.reason: Patients can go undiagnosed for decades, leading to personal and family uncertainty, high levels of stress, decreased productivity, limited accessibility to disease-specific counseling and resources, decreased quality of life, and excess utilization of medical services.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · UNDIAGNOSED RARE DISEASES | THE EFFECT OF THE UNDIAGNOSED DISEASE STATE ON THE PATIENT | APPROACH TO CHALLENGING DISEASES OF UNKNOWN ETIOLOGY | COMPREHENSIVE DATA COLLECTION

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.assertion: Complementary screening tools like metabolomics can enhance diagnostic accuracy.
items.reason: Metabolomics, when combined with sequencing data, generates a fuller picture of disease manifestations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.subtopic: VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS
items.assertion: Testing strategies based on historical data are always sufficient for diagnosing congenital disorders of glycosylation.
items.reason: Historical testing methods like transferrin isoelectric focusing have been replaced by more advanced techniques.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because historical methods may not be sufficient; the reason is true as newer approaches are needed.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · VALIDATION OF SUBJECTIVE AND OBJECTIVE FINDINGS

items.assertion: A systematic exposure history is crucial for evaluating patients with undiagnosed diseases.
items.reason: Techniques for measuring the exposome are in early stages but evolving rapidly to capture complex exposures.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why a systematic exposure history is crucial; it explains the state of exposome measurement techniques.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.assertion: The traditional approach to focused external exposure assessment provides an integrated measure of all exposures over a lifetime.
items.reason: Traditional methods do not capture the totality of human exposure, which is necessary for chronic disease risk assessment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because traditional approaches do not provide an integrated measure; the reason is true.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · EXPOSOME

items.assertion: Patients with undiagnosed diseases often face more disabling factors than the primary illness itself.
items.reason: These include uncertainty, loss of abilities, work, relationships, autonomy, and financial security.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these factors can be equally or more disabling than the primary illness.
items.reference: Novel Approaches to Diseases of Unknown Etiology · CHALLENGES | CONCLUSION
